**Interdisciplinarity** or **interdisciplinary studies** involves the combination of multiple [academic disciplines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academic_discipline "Academic discipline") into one activity (e.g., a research project).[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdisciplinarity#cite_note-1) It draws knowledge from several fields such as sociology, anthropology, psychology, economics, etc. It is related to an _[interdiscipline](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdiscipline "Interdiscipline")_ or an _interdisciplinary field,_ which is an organizational unit that crosses traditional boundaries between [academic disciplines](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Outline_of_academic_disciplines "Outline of academic disciplines") or [schools of thought](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/School_of_thought "School of thought"), as new needs and professions emerge. Large [engineering](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Engineering "Engineering") teams are usually interdisciplinary, as a [power station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Power_station "Power station") or [mobile phone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") or other project requires the melding of several specialties. However, the term "interdisciplinary" is sometimes confined to academic settings.

The term _interdisciplinary_ is applied within education and training pedagogies to describe studies that use methods and insights of several established disciplines or traditional fields of study. Interdisciplinarity involves researchers, students, and teachers in the goals of connecting and integrating several academic schools of thought, professions, or technologies—along with their specific perspectives—in the pursuit of a common task. The [epidemiology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epidemiology "Epidemiology") of [HIV/AIDS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS") or [global warming](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") requires understanding of diverse disciplines to solve complex problems. _Interdisciplinary_ may be applied where the subject is felt to have been neglected or even misrepresented in the traditional disciplinary structure of research institutions, for example, [women's studies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Women%27s_studies "Women's studies") or ethnic area studies. Interdisciplinarity can likewise be applied to complex subjects that can only be understood by combining the perspectives of two or more fields.

The [adjective](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Adjective "Adjective")_interdisciplinary_ is most often used in educational circles when researchers from two or more disciplines pool their approaches and modify them so that they are better suited to the problem at hand, including the case of the team-taught course where students are required to understand a given subject in terms of multiple traditional disciplines. Interdisciplinary education fosters cognitive flexibility and prepares students to tackle complex, real-world problems by integrating knowledge from multiple fields. This approach emphasizes active learning, critical thinking, and problem-solving skills, equipping students with the adaptability needed in an increasingly interconnected world.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdisciplinarity#cite_note-2) For example, the subject of [land use](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Land_use "Land use") may appear differently when examined by different disciplines, for instance, [biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biology "Biology"), [chemistry](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemistry "Chemistry"), [economics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economics "Economics"), [geography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Geography "Geography"), and [politics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Politics "Politics").

Development
-----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Interdisciplinarity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Development")]

Although "interdisciplinary" and "interdisciplinarity" are frequently viewed as twentieth century terms, the concept has historical antecedents, most notably [Greek philosophy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greek_philosophy "Greek philosophy").[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdisciplinarity#cite_note-ausburg-3)[Julie Thompson Klein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julie_Thompson_Klein "Julie Thompson Klein") attests that "the roots of the concepts lie in a number of ideas that resonate through modern discourse—the ideas of a unified science, general knowledge, synthesis and the integration of knowledge",[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdisciplinarity#cite_note-4) while Giles Gunn says that [Greek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece")[historians](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Historians "Historians") and [dramatists](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Playwright "Playwright") took elements from other realms of knowledge (such as [medicine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Medicine "Medicine") or [philosophy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy")) to further understand their own material.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdisciplinarity#cite_note-5) The building of [Roman roads](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roman_roads "Roman roads") required men who understood [surveying](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Surveying "Surveying"), [material science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Material_science "Material science"), [logistics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Logistics "Logistics") and several other disciplines. Any broadminded humanist project involves interdisciplinarity, and history shows a crowd of cases, as seventeenth-century Leibniz's task to create a system of universal justice, which required linguistics, economics, management, ethics, law philosophy, politics, and even sinology.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interdisciplinarity#cite_note-6)

Interdisciplinary programs sometimes arise from a shared conviction that the traditional disciplines are unable or unwilling to address an important problem. For example, social science disciplines such as [anthropology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthropology "Anthropology") and [sociology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") paid little attention to the social analysis of [technology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Technology "Technology") throughout most of the twentieth century. As a result, many social scientists with interests in technology have joined [science, technology and society](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Science,_technology_and_society "Science, technology and society") programs, which are typically staffed by scholars drawn from numerous disciplines. They may also arise from new research developments, such as [nanotechnology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology"), which cannot be addressed without combining the approaches of two or more disciplines. Examples include [quantum information processing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_information_processing "Quantum information processing"), an amalgamation of [quantum physics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantum_physics "Quantum physics") and [computer science](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), and [bioinformatics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bioinformatics "Bioinformatics"), combining [molecular biology](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology") with computer science. [Sustainable development](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development") as a research area deals with problems requiring analysis and synthesis across economic, social and environmental spheres; often an integration of multiple social and natural science disciplines. enriched_points.expanded_content: Interdisciplinary methods have evolved significantly over the past decades, adapting to complex global challenges by integrating insights from diverse academic fields to enhance research applicability.

Research now incorporates diverse methodologies, combining qualitative and quantitative techniques, fostering innovation and collaboration across disciplines to address multifaceted societal issues effectively.

enriched_points.point_title: Role of mixed methods in enhancing understanding
enriched_points.expanded_content: Mixed methods leverage the strengths of both qualitative and quantitative research, providing a comprehensive understanding of complex phenomena, thereby enriching data interpretation and decision-making processes.
